summary: The codebase demonstrates strong security practices across all major attack surfaces. YAML parsing uses safe_load everywhere, SQL queries are fully parameterized with a column-name whitelist on dynamic updates, the web GUI has bearer token authentication and security headers, and the expression evaluator is a restricted DSL with no eval/exec. Nine non-blocking findings remain, primarily around defense-in-depth hardening for shell command interpolation, SSRF in the API step, and CSP tightening.

findings.title: Shell command interpolation from workflow inputs with no sanitization
findings.description: In cli_step.py line 58, user-supplied workflow inputs are interpolated directly into shell commands via the evaluator's interpolate() function. The resolved command is then passed to subprocess.run with shell=True (in the default/cmd case) or as an argument to bash -c. A workflow command like 'echo ${inputs.name}' will have the input value substituted verbatim. If a workflow author uses unsanitized external input in a command template and an attacker controls that input, shell metacharacter injection is possible. This is partially by-design for a workflow engine, but there is no mechanism to warn authors or escape interpolated values.
findings.recommendation: Document the trust model prominently: workflow authors are responsible for input sanitization when interpolating into shell commands. Consider offering a shlex.quote() wrapper function accessible in expressions, or an optional 'sanitize: true' flag on CLI steps that auto-escapes interpolated values.

findings.title: API step allows requests to arbitrary URLs including internal network
findings.description: The api_step.py validates that the URL scheme is http or https (line 31) but does not restrict requests to internal/private IP ranges (127.0.0.1, 10.x.x.x, 169.254.169.254, etc.). A workflow that interpolates user input into the URL field could be used for SSRF, probing internal services or accessing cloud metadata endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Consider blocking well-known metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) by default. Add an optional deny-list for private IP ranges. At minimum, document the SSRF risk for workflow authors who interpolate user input into API step URLs.

findings.title: Auth token printed to console and exposed in browser URL bar
findings.description: In server.py lines 203-204, the full auth token is printed to stdout and passed as a query parameter when opening the browser. The token appears in terminal scrollback, browser address bar, browser history, and may be logged by HTTP proxies. The token is also accepted via query parameter (line 86), which is less secure than header-only auth.
findings.recommendation: Consider a token-exchange flow: the initial URL contains a one-time code that sets an HttpOnly session cookie, then subsequent API calls use the cookie. This keeps the long-lived token out of URL history and proxy logs.

findings.title: No CORS configuration on the web API
findings.description: The Starlette application does not configure CORS middleware. While the server binds to localhost only and requires a bearer token, a malicious webpage could attempt cross-origin requests to localhost:8420. The token requirement mitigates exploitation, but adding explicit CORS restrictions provides defense-in-depth. Simple requests (GET without custom headers) bypass preflight, though they would still need the token.
findings.recommendation: Add CORSMiddleware with allow_origins restricted to http://localhost:8420 (or the actual origin). This ensures browsers block cross-origin requests at the preflight stage.

findings.title: Content-Security-Policy allows unsafe-inline for scripts
findings.description: The CSP header includes 'unsafe-inline' in script-src (line 35). This weakens XSS protection -- any injected inline script would execute. While the GUI serves pre-built static assets and API responses are JSON, this should be tightened if possible.
findings.recommendation: If the frontend build outputs only external JS files, remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. If inline scripts are required by the build tooling, use nonce-based CSP with a per-request nonce.

findings.title: workflow_ref step accepts arbitrary file paths without restriction
findings.description: WorkflowRefStep.workflow_ref becomes Path(step.workflow_ref) with no path traversal checks (workflow_ref_step.py line 23). A workflow could reference files anywhere on the filesystem. While this would fail YAML parsing for non-workflow files, it could leak file existence information. The CLI tool runs with user privileges so there is no privilege escalation, but it diverges from the path traversal guards used elsewhere (loader.py, GUI API).
findings.recommendation: Apply path traversal checks consistent with loader.py (block '..' components). Consider restricting workflow_ref to paths relative to the parent workflow directory.

findings.title: No depth limit on recursive sub-workflow execution
findings.description: workflow_ref_step.py allows sub-workflows to reference other sub-workflows with no recursion depth limit. A circular or deeply nested workflow_ref chain would cause stack overflow or resource exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Add a maximum recursion depth counter (e.g., 10 levels) passed through RunContext. Reject execution if the limit is exceeded.

findings.title: Internal error details exposed in envelope messages
findings.description: In runner.py lines 238 and 365, Python exception messages (which may contain file paths, database errors, or stack details) are included verbatim in the envelope output via 'Internal error: {exc}'. The GUI API also returns these to HTTP clients.
findings.recommendation: Log the full exception internally while returning a generic error message in the envelope. Offer a --verbose flag for CLI debugging that includes details.

findings.title: SQLite database and run artifacts created with default permissions
findings.description: The ~/.checkpointflow directory and its contents are created with default OS permissions (store.py line 85). On multi-user Unix systems this could expose workflow data to other users. On the primary target platform (single-user Windows), default ACLs on the user home directory provide adequate protection.
findings.recommendation: On Unix, set the base directory to mode 0o700 on creation. On Windows the current behavior is acceptable.
